chapters.title: How Magicians Collect Virtues from the Three-Fold World

passages.text: The whole power of Magic consists of three parts: the world elementary, the world celestial, and the world intellectual. He who shall know all these three parts well, and know how to apply them to each other, shall be a true Magician.

For seeing that man is composed of three parts — body, soul, and spirit — there must be a three-fold knowledge: of the elementary world for the body, of the celestial for the soul, and of the intellectual or divine for the spirit.

Now natural philosophy teaches what are the virtues of things, and what occult properties they have — not manifest to the senses, but hidden in nature's secret operations, to be found by experience and reason.

chapters.title: Of the Four Elements, their Qualities, and Mutual Mixtions

passages.text: The principles of all corporal things are the four elements: Fire, Earth, Water, and Air. All philosophers do steadfastly affirm that these four elements are the first matter of all sublunar things.
Fire is hot and dry; Earth is cold and dry; Water is cold and moist; Air is hot and moist. Thus do they oppose one another by contraries: Fire is contrary to Water, and Earth contrary to Air.

The order of the elements is this: Earth is lowest, then Water, then Air, then Fire — each interpenetrating the other, each lending its virtue to the other, until through mutual mixture all things come to be.

Now the qualities of the elements are two in number to each element: one proper and one common to the next element. Fire shares heat with Air, Air shares moisture with Water, Water shares cold with Earth, Earth shares dryness with Fire.

chapters.title: Of the Celestial Virtue of Stars and Signs

passages.text: After the elementary virtues, we must consider the celestial. The heavens are not only cause of good or bad fortune to men through their influence, but they also seal upon sublunar things their particular natures.
Every star hath its peculiar nature, property and condition — the quality of which it doth render and impart, as well to the Sun, as to the Moon, and also to all inferior things by natural means.

There are three orders of celestial knowledge: the simple light of the fixed stars, the composite light gathered through the planets, and the total synthesis achieved when the skilled magician aligns all three worlds.

chapters.title: Of the Intelligences and Spirits That Are Over the Elements

passages.text: Above and beyond the elements and stars are the intelligences — divine beings that govern each sphere. Trismegistus named these the Daemons; the Platonists called them Gods of the second rank; the Hebrews call them Angelic Princes.

As God governs the angels, so the angels rule the stars, the stars rule the elements, and through the elements they rule all lower bodies. This is the great chain of being, and through it the Magician works his art.

The names of the intelligences over the four quarters of the world are: Oriens in the East, Paymon in the West, Egyn in the North, and Amaymon in the South. These are the governors of the elemental spirits.
